items.question_text: How are ATS route letters spoken when describing a route such as R20 or A50?

choices.text: State the route letters phonetically, followed by the route number in group form.
State the route letters as plain-English letter names, followed by each separate digit.
State only the route number because the route letters are implied by the chart.
State the route letters phonetically, then the route number digit by digit.

items.explanation: ATS route letters are spoken phonetically and the route number is spoken in group form.

items.situation: You need to state the route designator for North American Route 67 Bravo. Build the phraseology.

items.question_text: When must the alternate direction be added after a Victor airway number?

choices.text: When describing a VOR, VORTAC, or TACAN alternate airway.
When describing any standard Victor airway, even without an alternate direction.
Only when the route is a military training route.
Only when the route number has more than two digits.

items.explanation: A VOR, VORTAC, or TACAN alternate airway uses Victor, the airway number in group form, and the alternate direction.

items.situation: A controller wants to clear an aircraft via A50 and plans to say "A fifty" because the pilot is domestic and the route appears clearly on the strip.
items.para_context: ATS routes use phonetic route letters followed by the route number in group form. Low-altitude published RNAV routes also use the route letter phonetically followed by the route number in group form.

choices.text: State the route letter phonetically, such as "Alfa Fifty."
Say "A fifty" because phonetics are optional for domestic ATS routes.
Drop the route letter and state only "Fifty."
Say each numeral separately as "Alfa five zero."

items.explanation: ATS route letters are spoken phonetically, not as plain letter names.

items.question_text: How should a NAVAID be stated when it is used as a clearance limit and the type is known?

choices.text: State the name of the NAVAID followed by the type of NAVAID.
State only the NAVAID name because the type is always implied.
State only the location identifier because that is more precise than the name.
State the NAVAID frequency instead of the NAVAID type.

items.explanation: When a NAVAID is the clearance limit and the type is known, include the NAVAID type after the name.

items.situation: You need to describe a 20-mile arc southwest of Kirksville VOR. Build the phraseology.
items.target_phrase: TWO ZERO MILE ARC SOUTHWEST OF KIRKSVILLE VOR

items.question_text: How must a nondirectional beacon reference be described?

choices.text: State the course to or bearing from the beacon, then the beacon name, then the words radio beacon.
State only the beacon name and the course, because the words radio beacon are redundant.
State the magnetic heading, the beacon frequency, and the associated airport.
State the course and the runway served by the beacon.

items.explanation: Nondirectional beacon references include the course to or bearing from the beacon, the beacon name, and the words radio beacon.

items.situation: You are issuing a clearance limit to Grand Rapids, and the type of NAVAID is known. Another controller says just saying "Grand Rapids" is enough because the pilot will know which facility you mean.
items.para_context: When a NAVAID is used as a clearance limit, state the name of the NAVAID followed by the type of NAVAID if the type is known.

choices.text: State the NAVAID name followed by the type, such as "Grand Rapids VOR."
State only "Grand Rapids" because a known fix name never needs the type.
State only the location identifier because it is shorter than the NAVAID name.
Avoid using the NAVAID at all and substitute the nearest airport name.

items.explanation: A clearance limit uses the NAVAID name and type when the type is known.

items.question_text: How should an unnamed fix based on a radial and distance be described?

choices.text: State the NAVAID name, the specified radial, localizer, or azimuth, the distance in miles, and the phrase mile fix.
State only the airway crossing nearest the fix and let the pilot infer the rest.
State the airport name and the mileage from the field without the NAVAID reference.
State the NAVAID name and the mileage only, because the radial is optional.

items.explanation: An unnamed fix uses the NAVAID name, the specified radial, localizer, or azimuth, the mileage, and the words mile fix.

items.situation: You need to state the Appleton 050 radial 37-mile fix. Build the fix description.
items.target_phrase: APPLETON ZERO FIVE ZERO RADIAL THREE SEVEN MILE FIX

items.question_text: If a fix is charted on a SID, STAR, en route chart, or approach plate, how must it be stated?

choices.text: State the name of the fix.
State the nearest airway and mileage from the NAVAID.
State the NAVAID name and radial even when the fix name is charted.
State the runway associated with the procedure first.

items.explanation: When the fix is charted, use the fix name rather than restating the underlying radial-and-distance components.

items.situation: A controller says an aircraft is "passing Victor Twelve" to describe the aircraft's position relative to a fix on the route.
items.para_context: Use specific terms to describe a fix. Do not use expressions such as passing Victor Twelve or passing J Eleven.

choices.text: Use a specific fix description or the charted fix name instead of saying "passing Victor Twelve."
Keep using the airway name because it is shorter than a fix description.
Use only the mileage from the departure airport and omit the fix reference.
Describe the position by sector boundary instead of by fix.

items.explanation: A route name is not a substitute for a specific fix description.

items.question_text: How is a named route such as NAR 67B stated?
items.explanation: Named routes are stated as North American Route or Bahama Route followed by the route number in group form.

choices.text: North American Route Sixty-Seven Bravo.
NAR Six Seven Bravo.
North American Route Six Seven Bravo.
November Alfa Romeo Sixty-Seven Bravo.

items.question_text: Complete the prescribed route designator: "NORTH AMERICAN ROUTE SIXTY-SEVEN _____."
items.explanation: The approved named-route example is North American Route Sixty-Seven Bravo.

choices.text: BRAVO

items.question_text: How is low-altitude published RNAV route T210 stated?
items.explanation: Low-altitude published RNAV routes use the route letter phonetically followed by the number in group form.

choices.text: Tango Two Ten.
T Two One Zero.
Tango Two One Zero.
Tee Two Ten.

items.question_text: Published high-altitude RNAV routes are stated with the letter Q followed by the route number in group form.
items.explanation: High-altitude published RNAV routes use the letter Q followed by the route number in group form.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: How should IR 531 be spoken?
items.explanation: Military training routes use the letters I-R or V-R followed by the route number in group form.

choices.text: I-R Five Thirty-One.
I R Five Three One.
India Romeo Five Thirty-One.
I-R Five Three One.

items.question_text: When a NAVAID is used as a clearance limit and the type is known, how should it be stated?
items.explanation: A clearance-limit NAVAID uses the name followed by the NAVAID type if the type is known.

choices.text: State the NAVAID name followed by the type of NAVAID.
State only the NAVAID name.
State only the NAVAID frequency.
State the associated airport name instead of the NAVAID name.

items.question_text: Complete the clearance-limit phraseology: "CLEARED TO GRAND RAPIDS _____."
items.explanation: The approved example is Cleared to Grand Rapids VOR.

choices.text: VOR

items.question_text: How is a 20-mile arc southwest of Kirksville VOR described?
items.explanation: Arcs use the distance in miles, the words mile arc, the compass direction from the NAVAID, the word of, and the NAVAID name.

choices.text: Two Zero mile arc southwest of Kirksville VOR.
Kirksville VOR southwest arc two zero miles.
Southwest Kirksville arc twenty miles VOR.
Kirksville two zero southwest radial arc.

items.question_text: How is a quadrant within a radius of a NAVAID described?
items.explanation: State the quadrant direction and the distance in miles from the NAVAID.

choices.text: State the direction from the NAVAID in terms of the quadrant, followed by the distance in miles from the NAVAID.
State the runway served by the NAVAID and the mileage from the airport.
State the NAVAID frequency, then the inbound course, then the mileage.
State only the distance, because the quadrant is implied by the fix name.

items.question_text: A nondirectional beacon reference may omit the words radio beacon if the beacon name is already obvious.
items.explanation: Nondirectional beacon references include the words radio beacon.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When a NAVAID is used in routing, what may be stated for the NAVAID reference?
items.explanation: Routing may use the NAVAID name or the phonetic alphabet equivalent of the location identifier.

choices.text: The NAVAID name or the phonetic alphabet equivalent of its location identifier.
Only the associated airport name.
Only the NAVAID frequency.
Only the airway number that crosses the NAVAID.

items.question_text: If a fix is not named, how should it be stated?
items.explanation: An unnamed fix uses the NAVAID name, the specified radial, localizer, or azimuth, the distance in miles, and the phrase mile fix.

choices.text: State the NAVAID name, the specified radial, localizer, or azimuth, the distance in miles, and the phrase mile fix.
State only the mileage from the airport because the NAVAID reference is optional.
State the airway identifier and the mileage from the NAVAID.
State the chart name instead of the NAVAID reference.

items.question_text: Complete the unnamed-fix phraseology: "APPLETON ZERO FIVE ZERO RADIAL THREE SEVEN MILE _____."
items.explanation: The approved unnamed-fix phrase ends with mile fix.

choices.text: FIX

items.question_text: If a fix is charted on a SID, STAR, en route chart, or approach plate, how should it be described?
items.explanation: A charted fix is described by its fix name.

choices.text: State the name of the fix.
State the NAVAID name and radial even if the fix name is published.
State the airway number the fix lies on.
State the nearest airport name and distance.

items.question_text: It is acceptable to describe a fix by saying "passing Victor Twelve" if the pilot and controller both know the route.
items.explanation: Use specific fix terms instead of expressions such as passing Victor Twelve or passing J Eleven.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: Which transmission correctly states an unnamed localizer-based fix?
items.explanation: A localizer-based unnamed fix states the localizer or azimuth, the distance in miles, and the phrase mile fix.

choices.text: Reno localizer back course Four mile fix.
Reno Four mile localizer back course.
Four mile fix off Reno airway.
Reno localizer four miles to the runway.
